definition: seeming to be everywhere at once.
real_world: Reach for it when something has become so common it's hard to avoid — an app, a brand, a phrase.
example: Those QR-code menus became ubiquitous almost overnight.

definition: dealing with things sensibly and practically rather than idealistically.
real_world: Useful in work conversations to praise a realistic choice over a perfect-but-impractical one.
example: Let's be pragmatic — ship the simple version now and improve it later.

definition: honest, straightforward expression.
real_world: Name the quality you want (or appreciate) in a hard conversation or honest feedback.
example: I appreciated her candor about why the project slipped.

definition: very weak or flimsy; barely holding together.
real_world: A polite way to push back on a shaky argument or a stretch of a connection.
example: The link between those two events is pretty tenuous.

definition: briefly and clearly expressed.
real_world: Ask for or praise brevity in emails, docs, and meetings.
example: Can you give me the succinct version before the call?

definition: most noticeable or important.
real_world: Steer a rambling discussion back to what actually matters.
example: The salient point is that we run out of runway by March.

definition: acting with care and foresight; sensible.
real_world: Justify a cautious decision about money, risk, or timing without sounding fearful.
example: It's prudent to keep three months of expenses saved.

definition: a subtle difference or shade of meaning.
real_world: Signal that a topic isn't black-and-white before you weigh in.
example: There's a lot of nuance here that the headline misses.

definition: sociable and fond of company.
real_world: A warm, precise way to describe an outgoing person.
example: He's gregarious — he'll know half the room within ten minutes.

definition: stated or appearing to be true, but perhaps not the real reason.
real_world: Politely flag a suspected hidden motive.
example: The ostensible reason for the meeting was planning, but it felt like a review.

definition: to make a bad situation better or less painful.
real_world: Sound precise when talking about easing a problem you can't fully fix.
example: Better lighting won't end the crime but it may ameliorate it.

definition: to place side by side, usually for contrast.
real_world: Handy when comparing ideas, designs, or sets of numbers.
example: Juxtapose last year's figures with this year's and the trend jumps out.

definition: to use vague language deliberately to avoid committing.
real_world: Call out (or consciously avoid) a dodgy non-answer.
example: Stop equivocating — are we doing this or not?

definition: generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival.
real_world: Describe gracious behavior, particularly after winning or after conflict.
example: She was magnanimous in victory and thanked her opponent.

definition: overly concerned with tiny details or formal rules.
real_world: Gently flag nitpicking — often used to soften your own correction.
example: I don't want to be pedantic, but it's 'fewer,' not 'less.'

definition: clear, logical, and convincing.
real_world: High praise for a well-built argument.
example: He made a cogent case for delaying the launch.

definition: lasting for a very short time.
real_world: Adds weight when talking about fleeting things — trends, moments, attention.
example: Internet fame is ephemeral; here today, forgotten next week.

definition: using very few words.
real_world: Describe someone terse — or aim for it yourself when less is more.
example: His laconic 'fine' told me it was not, in fact, fine.

definition: very attentive to detail and cleanliness; hard to please.
real_world: Describe a meticulous person or an exacting standard.
example: She's fastidious about her code — every variable named just so.

definition: cautious; weighing all the consequences before acting.
real_world: Justify holding back judgment or information until you know more.
example: Be circumspect about what you share before the deal closes.

definition: to shock or excite someone into taking action.
real_world: Name the moment that finally spurs a person or group to move.
example: The looming deadline finally galvanized the team.

definition: based on random choice rather than reason.
real_world: Challenge a rule or number that seems made up.
example: The 200-word limit feels completely arbitrary.

definition: extremely careful and precise.
real_world: Strong, specific praise for thorough work.
example: His meticulous notes saved us when the server crashed.

definition: harmless; unlikely to offend or cause trouble.
real_world: Describe something that seems harmless — sometimes deceptively so.
example: It was an innocuous question that quietly changed the whole interview.

definition: reluctant to speak about one's thoughts or feelings.
real_world: More precise than 'shy' for someone who holds back.
example: He's reticent about his past, so don't push it.

definition: real and concrete; able to be clearly seen or measured.
real_world: Demand real results over vague promises.
example: I want tangible progress by Friday, not just plans.

definition: a typical model or pattern of how something works or is thought about.
real_world: Sounds sharp when describing a fundamental shift in how things are done.
example: Remote work was a genuine paradigm shift for the company.

definition: sharp, perceptive, and good at judging situations.
real_world: A clean compliment for someone's insight.
example: That was an astute read of the room.

definition: to make something less severe or harmful.
real_world: The precise word for reducing risk or damage — common in work and finance.
example: We bought insurance to mitigate the risk.

definition: understood by only a small group with specialized knowledge.
real_world: Describe niche knowledge without sounding dismissive.
example: The talk got esoteric fast — I lost the thread after slide three.

definition: open and willing to agree or cooperate.
real_world: Test the waters before proposing something: are they amenable to it?
example: She seemed amenable to pushing the meeting to next week.
